items.question: How do you load a CSV without letting pandas infer dtypes?
items.answer: Pass an explicit dtype dict to read_csv (and parse_dates for timestamps), optionally usecols to limit columns. After load, verify with df.dtypes and df.head() rather than assuming inference is correct.

items.question: When would you choose Parquet over CSV for an ML dataset?
items.answer: When files are large or wide, dtypes must be preserved, and you need selective column reads. Parquet is columnar, compressed, and typed; CSV requires full text parsing and has no embedded schema.

items.question: How can loading choices reduce memory usage before modeling?
items.answer: Use usecols/columns, specify smaller dtypes (float32, int32, category), parse dates once, and avoid object columns for repeated strings. Audit with memory_usage(deep=True) immediately after load.

items.question: Is loading the full dataset before a train/test split always a leakage risk?
items.answer: No—the read itself is fine if you partition immediately and fit preprocessors on train only. Leakage occurs when statistics or label-derived transforms use validation or test rows before or during training.

items.question: How do you load nested JSON logs into a flat DataFrame?
items.answer: Use read_json with lines=True for JSONL, then pd.json_normalize to expand nested keys into columns. Assign dtypes (category, string) on stable fields early to control memory.
